summary: Gruber surveys the landscape of third-party iMessage clients — Beeper Cloud, Texts, and the ill-fated Sunbird/Nothing Chat — before focusing on Beeper Mini, an Android app that reverse-engineered iMessage's protocol. He correctly predicted Apple would shut it down within days of launch, and argues Apple was justified in doing so. Gruber rejects the notion that Apple is ethically obligated to open iMessage to Android, noting that iMessage's costs are subsidized by Apple device sales. While he personally wishes Apple had released an Android iMessage client a decade ago, he sees Beeper Mini's business model as fundamentally unsustainable and presumptuous.
key_points: Beeper Mini reverse-engineered iMessage using a 16-year-old's work, but Apple shut it down within two days of launch by closing the loopholes it exploited
Giving iCloud credentials to third-party relay servers (as Beeper Cloud and Sunbird required) is a terrible security practice, as the Sunbird/Nothing Chat privacy disaster demonstrated
Apple has no ethical obligation to open iMessage to Android — it's a value-add for Apple devices, subsidized by hardware sales, not a public utility
Beeper Mini's business model presumed Apple should foot the server bill for Beeper's paying customers, treating iMessage as a public resource
Texts is the only reasonable approach among these services, communicating locally with Apple's Messages app without relay servers or credential sharing
money_quotes: If you're thinking that giving your iCloud account credentials to a third party so that they can sign you into iMessage on a virtual MacOS machine in the cloud sounds like a sketchy idea, you're correct. It's a terrible idea.
It's untenable that there's unsanctioned client software for a messaging platform for which privacy and security are a primary feature.
If Apple wanted an iMessage client for Android they'd release an iMessage client for Android.
Seems irresponsible for Beeper to charge a subscription for an unsupported service.
That last sentence translates to 'We'll keep closing loopholes if Beeper Mini finds another workaround.'

The whole business model for Beeper Mini presupposed that Apple should just foot the bill for the usage of Beeper's (paying!) customers, as though iMessage is a public resource, or part of your cellular phone service, like SMS/MMS/RCS.
Apple releasing an iMessage client for Android would be a pleasant surprise; Apple opening a third-party iMessage API would be shocking.
What Migicovsky is implicitly arguing is that Apple is obliged to make E2EE messaging as seamless for Android users as they've made it for iPhone users.
key_insight: Beeper Mini was a clever hack built on a fundamentally untenable premise — that Apple would tolerate unauthorized third-party access to a messaging platform it funds, controls, and markets on the basis of privacy and security.
